rules.title: Win32 App Detection Script Failure
rules.description: Detects when a Win32 app detection script fails or returns unexpected results.
rules.explanation: A Win32 app detection script failed or did not detect the app after installation. This causes the app to appear as 'not installed' despite successful installation.

remediation.title: Fix detection rules
steps: Review the app's detection rules in Intune
Verify file/registry paths are correct
Test detection script manually on a device

rules.title: App Installation Error
rules.description: Detects app installation failures with specific exit codes or HRESULT error codes and suggests fixes.
rules.explanation: An app installation failed with a specific error code. Common MSI exit codes: 1603 (Fatal error during installation), 1618 (Another installation already in progress), 1619 (Package could not be opened), 1625 (Installation prohibited by policy). Common HRESULT codes are also captured.

remediation.title: Investigate the error code
steps: Check the error code in the rule finding details
Look up the specific error code in Microsoft documentation
Review the IME logs on the device: C:\ProgramData\Microsoft\IntuneManagementExtension\Logs\IntuneManagementExtension.log
Verify all prerequisites for the app are met
Check if another installation was running concurrently (error 1618)

rules.title: App Dependency Chain Failure
rules.description: Detects when an app fails because a dependency app failed to install.
rules.explanation: An app installation failed because one of its dependency apps failed to install. Fix the dependency first, then retry.

remediation.title: Fix dependency chain
steps: Identify which dependency app failed
Fix the dependency app first
Verify dependency chain order in Intune

rules.title: Insufficient Disk Space
rules.description: Detects when disk space is critically low during enrollment, which can cause app installation failures.
rules.explanation: Performance monitoring shows disk free space dropped below 5 GB during enrollment. This can cause app installation failures, especially for large Win32 apps.

remediation.title: Free disk space
steps: Check device disk size meets minimum requirements
Clean up temporary files and IME cache (C:\Windows\IMECache)
Consider using a larger disk image

rules.title: Content Download Timeout
rules.description: Detects when app content download times out or fails.
rules.explanation: App content download timed out or failed. This is usually caused by network issues, proxy interference, or CDN problems. The IME log shows a Delivery Optimization timeout or download content error.

remediation.title: Improve download speed
steps: Check network bandwidth
Verify proxy doesn't block *.delivery.mp.microsoft.com
Consider Delivery Optimization configuration

rules.title: Reboot Required Loop
rules.description: Detects when the same app installation is attempted 3 or more times, indicating a reboot-required loop.
rules.explanation: The same app has been installed 3 or more times during this enrollment. This typically indicates the app requires a reboot between install attempts, but the ESP is not honoring the reboot request, causing an install-detect-retry loop.

remediation.title: Fix reboot handling
steps: Check if the app's return code mapping includes reboot codes (3010, 1641)
Verify ESP reboot behavior settings
Consider setting the app to 'Hard reboot' return code in Intune

rules.title: Multiple App Installation Failures
rules.description: Detects when the app tracking summary reports multiple failed app installations.
rules.explanation: The app tracking summary reports 2 or more failed app installations during enrollment. This suggests a systemic issue rather than an isolated app failure.

remediation.title: Review failed apps
steps: Check the individual app_install_failed events for specific error patterns
Look for a common pattern (all MSI apps, all large apps, all from a specific publisher)
Consider whether a shared prerequisite or dependency is failing

rules.title: Slow App Installation
rules.description: Detects app installations that take longer than 10 minutes.
rules.explanation: At least one app installation took longer than 10 minutes. This can indicate slow content delivery, installation overhead, or endpoint performance bottlenecks.

remediation.title: Analyze slow app behavior
steps: Identify the affected app from matched evidence in the rule result
Check network throughput and proxy impact during app download
Consider moving large/non-critical apps out of ESP blocking scope

rules.title: App Reverted to Error After Successful Install
rules.description: Detects when a specific app (matched by appId) was first reported as successfully installed but subsequently reported an IME enforcement error — while enrollment completed successfully. The app may be broken post-enrollment.
rules.explanation:
An app was initially reported as **successfully installed** (`app_install_completed`) but the IME (Intune Management Extension) subsequently reported an **Error** state for the same app via an `IME-ERROR-REPORT` event — while the overall enrollment completed successfully.

This pattern occurs when the IME re-evaluates enforcement state after the initial install report and detects a failure, e.g. `EnforcementState` transitions from `Success` → `Error`. The affected app and its details are captured in the matched evidence.

Common causes:
- A post-install detection check fails (detection rule mismatch)
- The enforcement error code `-2016345060` (0x87D13B9C) indicates the app state diverged from expected
- The app may appear installed in the ESP summary but is in an error state on the device

**The enrollment succeeded, but the affected app requires attention.**

remediation.title: Investigate the app's post-install enforcement state
steps: Note the appId and appName from the rule finding — these identify the exact affected app
Check the Intune portal: Devices > [Device] > Apps — confirm the current enforcement state of the app
Review IME logs on the device: C:\ProgramData\Microsoft\IntuneManagementExtension\Logs\IntuneManagementExtension.log
Search the log for the appId and look for 'EnforcementState' transitions (Success → Error) near the enrollment time
Error -2016345060 (0x87D13B9C) typically indicates a detection failure after install — verify the app's detection rule in Intune
Trigger a manual sync (Company Portal or Settings > Accounts > Access work or school > Sync) to force re-evaluation

remediation.title: Fix detection or installation issues
steps: If detection fails: correct the detection rule (file path, registry key, or PowerShell script) in the Intune app configuration
If the app itself is broken: redeploy or reassign the app to trigger a fresh install
Consider adding a post-install validation script to catch this scenario proactively

rules.title: Enforcement Error Resolved by Successful Detection
rules.description: Detects when an app reported an enforcement error (unmapped exit code) but was subsequently detected as installed. The error is informational — the app installed despite the unmapped exit code.
rules.explanation:
An app installation initially reported an **enforcement error** (unmapped exit code), but the IME detection check subsequently found the app **is installed**.

This happens when the MSI/EXE installer returns a non-standard exit code (e.g., 1603) that has no mapping configured in the Intune app definition. The IME treats unmapped exit codes as errors in its enforcement state, but when detection runs afterward, it confirms the app is present on the device.

**The app is installed and functional.** The enforcement error is an Intune app model artifact, not a real installation failure.

remediation.title: Configure exit code mapping in Intune
steps: Open the Intune portal > Apps > [affected app] > Properties > Edit
In the Return codes section, add a mapping for the reported exit code
Map the exit code to 'Success' or 'Soft reboot' depending on the installer behavior
This prevents the enforcement error from appearing in future deployments

relatedDocs.title: Win32 app return codes in Intune

rules.title: App Detection Failure During ESP (0x87D1041C)
rules.description: Detects when the Enrollment Status Page failed because Intune could not verify an app was installed (HRESULT 0x87D1041C). The installer ran but the detection rule did not match afterwards.

preconditions.description: Scope this rule to sessions where the ESP terminated against the Apps subcategory with the canonical detection-failure HRESULT. Works for any agent version, since esp_failure_settle_started is emitted by the V2 ProvisioningStatusTracker regardless of how the Installing-app promotion is later classified.

rules.explanation:
App **{{appName}}** failed during the Enrollment Status Page with HRESULT **{{errorCode}}** — *Application not detected after installation completed successfully*. The installer reported success but the Intune detection rule (file/registry/script) did not find the app afterwards, so the ESP Apps subcategory failed and the device's enrollment terminated.

This is a confirmed detection-rule mismatch, NOT an install timeout — the install itself most likely completed (or the installer exited 0). Common root causes:

- The detection rule looks for a registry/file path that the installer doesn't create (wrong path, wrong bitness — e.g. checking HKLM\SOFTWARE\... on a 32-bit installer that lands under WOW6432Node).
- The installer writes the detection artefact AFTER the ESP's detection probe runs (e.g. async post-install task, requires reboot).
- The detection script returns 0 with no stdout (Intune treats empty stdout as "not detected").
- The installer wrote files to a user profile but the rule looks in System scope (or vice-versa).

remediation.title: Validate the detection rule against an installed device
steps: Open Intune portal → Apps → {{appName}} → Properties → Detection rules
Note the exact path / registry key / script the rule checks
Install the app manually on a reference device (outside Autopilot) and verify the detection artefact actually exists at that path AFTER the installer finishes
For registry rules: check both HKLM\SOFTWARE\... and HKLM\SOFTWARE\WOW6432Node\... depending on the installer's bitness
For file rules: ensure the path is what the installer actually writes (case, trailing version folder, ProgramData vs Program Files)
For script detection: test the script in PowerShell as SYSTEM (psexec -s) — it must write at least one line to stdout AND exit 0

remediation.title: Investigate post-install delay
steps: Check whether the installer schedules an async task (Windows Installer scheduled reboot, service start) that creates the detection artefact only after ESP completes
If yes: either move the detection artefact creation into the synchronous installer path, or add a wait step in the installer wrapper script before exit
As a workaround, change the detection rule to something the synchronous installer writes immediately (typically the MSI ProductCode under HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Uninstall\)

remediation.title: Cross-check the agent's per-app event
steps: Look at the app_install_failed event in this session for DataJson.detectionResult — NotDetected confirms Intune's detection probe ran and returned negative
Look at app_install_failed.bytesDownloaded vs bytesTotal — if they match, the package downloaded fully (install error vs. download error)
Cross-reference DataJson.exitCode and hresultFromWin32 — these come from the installer itself and may explain why the detection artefact was not written

relatedDocs.title: Microsoft Learn: Troubleshoot Windows Autopilot ESP error codes
Win32 app detection rules in Intune

rules.title: Disk Space Exhaustion Caused Installation Failure
rules.description: Correlates declining disk free space with app installation failure, confirming insufficient storage as root cause.
rules.explanation:
**Root cause confirmed:** Performance monitoring shows disk free space dropped below 5 GB during enrollment, and apps failed to install. The device likely ran out of storage during app installation.

This is common when deploying many large Win32 apps to devices with small drives (64 GB or 128 GB).

remediation.title: Reduce disk consumption during enrollment
steps: Review total size of all apps deployed during ESP
Move non-critical apps to post-ESP deployment
Use MSIX or Store apps instead of Win32 where possible (smaller footprint)
Clean up IME cache: C:\Windows\IMECache

remediation.title: Use devices with larger drives
steps: Minimum 128 GB recommended for standard deployments
256 GB recommended when deploying large app suites (Office, Visual Studio, etc.)

rules.title: Proxy Configuration Causing Download Failure
rules.description: Correlates an active proxy configuration with app download failures, suggesting the proxy is blocking or throttling Intune content delivery.
rules.explanation:
A proxy (explicit or PAC-based) is configured on this device and app installations failed. Proxies can block or throttle downloads from *.delivery.mp.microsoft.com and other Intune CDN endpoints.

This is a common root cause when apps fail consistently on proxy-connected corporate networks.

remediation.title: Verify proxy bypass for Intune endpoints
steps: Add *.delivery.mp.microsoft.com to proxy bypass list
Add *.do.dsp.mp.microsoft.com (Delivery Optimization) to bypass list
Add login.microsoftonline.com and *.manage.microsoft.com to bypass list
Verify the PAC file is reachable at enrollment time (before user login)

remediation.title: Check proxy authentication
steps: Ensure the proxy does not require user authentication during OOBE/device phase
Consider using machine certificate-based proxy authentication
Test with proxy temporarily bypassed to confirm it is the root cause

rules.title: Windows Hello Provisioning Timeout
rules.description: Detects when Windows Hello for Business provisioning did not start within the expected timeframe after ESP completion.
rules.explanation: Windows Hello for Business provisioning did not start within the expected time after ESP exit. This may indicate that the Hello policy is not configured, the device doesn't meet prerequisites, or there's a connectivity issue with the Key Registration Service.

remediation.title: Verify Windows Hello configuration
steps: Check if Windows Hello for Business is configured in the tenant
Verify the policy targets the device/user correctly
Ensure the device has a TPM 2.0 (required for WHfB)
Check connectivity to the Key Registration Service (enterpriseregistration.windows.net)

rules.title: High Memory Usage During Enrollment
rules.description: Detects when memory usage exceeds 90% during enrollment, which can cause app installation failures or system instability.
rules.explanation: Memory usage exceeded 90% during enrollment. High memory pressure can cause app installation failures, system slowdowns, and ESP timeouts.

remediation.title: Reduce memory pressure
steps: Review which apps are being installed concurrently during ESP
Consider staggering resource-heavy installations
Check minimum RAM requirements for the device (8 GB recommended)
Investigate if background services are consuming excessive memory

rules.title: Unsupported Windows 11 Version
rules.description: Detects devices running Windows 11 version 22H2 or older, which is no longer supported by Microsoft.
rules.explanation: This device is running Windows 11 version 22H2 (Build 22621) or older, which has reached end of support. Microsoft no longer provides security updates or bug fixes for this version. Devices running unsupported OS versions are exposed to known vulnerabilities and may fail compliance checks.

remediation.title: Update to a supported Windows 11 version
steps: Verify the device meets hardware requirements for Windows 11 23H2 or 24H2
Update the Windows Autopilot deployment profile to use a supported OS image (23H2 or later)
If using a custom OS image, rebuild it with a supported Windows 11 version
For existing devices, deploy a Windows 11 feature update via Intune or Windows Update for Business

rules.title: Enrollment Failed
rules.description: Detects when the Autopilot enrollment has explicitly failed.
rules.explanation:
The Autopilot enrollment has explicitly failed.

**Key facts extracted from this session's events:**

- **Failure reason:** `{{reason}}`
- **First failed app:** `{{appName}}`
- **ESP failed subcategory:** `{{failedSubcategory}}`
- **ESP HRESULT:** `{{errorCode}}`

When an ESP Apps-subcategory failure is paired with HRESULT `0x87D1041C`, the device-side install most likely completed but the Intune detection rule did not match afterwards — see the **App Detection Failure During ESP** rule (ANALYZE-APP-013) in this same analysis for the actionable root cause and remediation steps. Any field still shown as a literal `{{…}}` placeholder was simply not recorded for this session — for example, a non-ESP failure path has no ESP failed-subcategory or HRESULT.

remediation.title: Investigate enrollment failure
steps: Start with the more specific ANALYZE-APP-* / ANALYZE-ESP-* rules that fired alongside this one — they correlate specific HRESULTs to actionable causes (e.g. 0x87D1041C → detection rule mismatch in ANALYZE-APP-013)
Read the `reason` field on the enrollment_failed event for the immediate trigger (e.g. esp_terminal_failure, hello_timeout, max_lifetime)
If a HRESULT is present, look it up against the ESP error catalog (Microsoft Learn: Autopilot ESP error codes)
Review other analyze rule results for correlated issues (disk space, network, app failures)
Check the device event timeline for the sequence of events leading to failure
Verify the Autopilot profile and ESP configuration in Intune

rules.title: Session Timed Out
rules.description: Detects when the enrollment session was force-failed by the server-side inactivity timeout (no agent telemetry for the configured SessionTimeoutHours window) without ever completing or explicitly failing.

preconditions.description: Suppress when the agent later reported a successful completion. The 5h server timeout can fire before the agent's own 6h max-lifetime, so a slow-but-successful session may complete after the sweep marked it timed out.
Suppress when an explicit enrollment_failed exists — that path is already covered by ANALYZE-ENRL-001; avoid double-reporting the same session.

rules.explanation:
The enrollment session was force-failed by the server-side maintenance sweep after `{{timeoutHours}}` hour(s) of agent inactivity — the device stopped sending telemetry and never reported completion or an explicit failure.

This usually means the agent process died or the device went offline mid-enrollment (power off, network loss, bluescreen, reimage) rather than a clean enrollment failure. Because there is no terminal `enrollment_failed` event, the more specific app/ESP rules cannot pinpoint a cause — the evidence simply stops at the last event before the gap.

remediation.title: Investigate the silent session
steps: Open the device event timeline and look at the LAST events before the gap — the final phase / app reached is where the device went silent
Check whether the device is still enrolled and online in Intune; a reimaged or powered-off device explains the silence
Look for previous_crash_detected / agent_shutting_down events earlier in the session that hint at an agent or OS crash
If the device is reachable, collect the agent + IME logs and re-run enrollment, correlating with the timeout timestamp
If many sessions time out in the same window, check for a tenant-wide network/connectivity issue or an Intune service incident

rules.title: ESP Blocking App Timeout
rules.description: Detects when the DeviceSetup phase of the ESP took longer than 30 minutes, indicating a stuck or very slow blocking app.
rules.explanation: The ESP DeviceSetup phase took more than 30 minutes. This indicates a blocking app is stuck installing, downloading very slowly, or has entered a retry loop.

remediation.title: Investigate blocking app
steps: Identify which app is blocking (check ESP events)
Verify app content is downloadable
Consider reducing the number of blocking apps

rules.title: ESP Subcategory Failed (Certificates)
rules.description: Detects when the Certificates subcategory of the Enrollment Status Page reported a failure, even if the enrollment later appears to have succeeded. Users can click 'Continue anyway' on an ESP error, which masks the underlying certificate provisioning failure — this rule surfaces the transition so it stays visible.
rules.author: Autopilot Monitor
rules.explanation:
The ESP Certificates subcategory transitioned to **failed** during enrollment. This is frequently masked when the user clicks 'Continue anyway' on the ESP error screen — the overall enrollment then appears successful while a required certificate (e.g. Wi-Fi, VPN, SCEP/PKCS) was never deployed.

The subcategory name is read from the registry (`CertificatesSubcategory`) and is language-invariant, so this rule fires regardless of OS display language.

remediation.title: Validate certificate delivery on the device
steps: Run `certutil -store My` and `certutil -store -user My` on the device to inspect installed certificates
Check `Event Viewer → Applications and Services Logs → Microsoft → Windows → DeviceManagement-Enterprise-Diagnostics-Provider → Admin` for SCEP/PKCS errors
Review the Intune portal for the device's certificate profile assignments and deployment status

remediation.title: Fix common certificate provisioning issues
steps: Verify the NDES/SCEP connector is online and healthy
Confirm the certificate template permissions grant 'Enroll' to the Intune service account / NDES computer account
Check that the device has network line-of-sight to the SCEP endpoint at the time of ESP
Re-sync the device (Settings → Accounts → Access work or school → Info → Sync) and re-check the Certificates subcategory

relatedDocs.title: Microsoft Learn: Troubleshoot certificate profiles in Intune
Microsoft Learn: Enrollment Status Page tracking

rules.title: Enrollment Duration Exceeded 60 Minutes
rules.description: Detects when the total Autopilot enrollment takes longer than 60 minutes.
rules.explanation: The Autopilot enrollment exceeded 60 minutes. This is unusually long and may indicate stuck apps, slow network, or policy conflicts. The default ESP timeout is 60 minutes, after which the enrollment may be marked as failed.

remediation.title: Investigate long enrollment
steps: Check the app installation timeline - identify which app took the longest
Review download speeds in the performance data
Consider reducing the number of ESP-blocking apps
Check if the ESP timeout policy is configured (default: 60 min)

rules.title: No Machine Certificates Found
rules.description: Detects when no certificates are present in the local machine personal store (LocalMachine\My). Missing certificates may indicate that MDM enrollment certificate provisioning or SCEP certificate deployment failed.
rules.explanation: The expected certificate with the configured subject was not found in the local machine certificate store (LocalMachine\My). Customize the 'value' field in the 'cert_subject_missing' condition to match your expected certificate subject (e.g. 'CN=Contoso Root CA'). The check is case-insensitive (substring match). If the certificate should have been provisioned via SCEP or MDM enrollment, this may indicate a deployment failure.

remediation.title: Verify the expected certificate
steps: Customize the analyze rule: change 'CN=YOUR-CERTIFICATE-SUBJECT' to your expected certificate subject (case-insensitive substring match)
Example subjects: 'CN=Contoso SCEP Cert', 'CN=Contoso Root CA', 'CN=MDM Device Certificate'
Check certlm.msc (Certificates - Local Computer > Personal > Certificates) manually to see what's present

remediation.title: Troubleshoot missing certificate
steps: Check if the device is properly enrolled in Intune (dsregcmd /status)
Verify SCEP/PKCS certificate profiles are assigned and targeted correctly
Check the NDES/SCEP connector status and reachability
Review IntuneManagementExtension logs for certificate enrollment errors
Ensure prerequisite certificates (Trusted Root CA) are deployed before the end-entity profile

templateVariables.name: cert_subject
templateVariables.label: Certificate Subject
templateVariables.description: The expected certificate subject in your environment (e.g., 'CN=Contoso Root CA'). Case-insensitive substring match against the local machine certificate store.

rules.title: Unexpected Local Admin Accounts Detected
rules.description: Detects when the LocalAdminAnalyzer flags unexpected local user accounts or profile directories on the device. This is a known Autopilot bypass technique where admin accounts are created before or during enrollment to gain persistent local admin access.
rules.author: Autopilot Monitor
rules.explanation:
The agent detected unexpected local user accounts or profile directories on this device that are not in the built-in or tenant-configured allowed list.

This is a security-relevant finding. Creating local admin accounts before or during Autopilot enrollment is a known bypass technique (e.g. via BypassNRO or Shift+F10 during OOBE) that grants persistent local administrator access.

Click the event link below to jump to the local_admin_analysis event and check: unexpected_accounts (local user accounts not on the allowed list), unexpected_profiles (C:\Users profile directories not on the allowed list), bypass_nro (whether the BypassNRO registry flag was set), and confidence_score (the agent's confidence in the finding, 0-100).

If these accounts are expected for your environment (e.g. a service account or helpdesk account), add them to the allowed list in Tenant Settings.

remediation.title: Review the unexpected accounts
steps: Open the session timeline and locate the local_admin_analysis event
Check the 'unexpected_accounts' and 'unexpected_profiles' lists in the event data
Verify whether these accounts are legitimate for your environment

remediation.title: Add expected accounts to the allowed list (false positive)
steps: Go to Settings → Agent Analyzers section
Under 'Allowed Admin Accounts', add the account names that are expected in your environment
Save the settings — future enrollments will no longer flag these accounts

remediation.title: Investigate a potential security issue
steps: Check if the BypassNRO registry flag was set (indicates OOBE was bypassed)
Review the enrollment timeline for Shift+F10 or other OOBE escape indicators
Check whether the unexpected account has admin group membership
Consider re-imaging the device if unauthorized admin access is confirmed

rules.title: Critical Vulnerability Detected
rules.description: Fires when the vulnerability correlation finds critical-severity CVEs or CISA KEV entries in installed software. This indicates the device has software with known, actively exploited vulnerabilities.
rules.author: Autopilot Monitor
rules.explanation: The device has installed software with critical vulnerabilities. Software with CVEs rated CRITICAL (CVSS >= 9.0) or listed in the CISA Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog was detected. These vulnerabilities are actively targeted by attackers and should be remediated immediately.

remediation.title: Review vulnerability findings
steps: Open the Vulnerability Report section in the session detail
Identify all software with critical or KEV-flagged vulnerabilities
Check if newer versions are available that address these CVEs
Update the affected software in your Intune app deployment

remediation.title: Update deployment packages
steps: Check Intune for outdated app package versions
Update the deployment package to the latest patched version
Re-deploy to affected devices

rules.title: Secure Boot UEFI CA 2023 certificate not deployed
rules.description: Detects devices where the Windows UEFI CA 2023 certificate has not been deployed to the Secure Boot DB. These devices will lose Secure Boot security update support when the 2011 certificates expire in June 2026.

preconditions.description: Skip on virtual machines — Secure Boot DB is host-controlled (Hyper-V/VMware/etc), the inside-the-VM UEFICA2023Status registry value is not the right indicator and not actionable for the VM admin.

rules.explanation:
This device has Secure Boot enabled but the **Windows UEFI CA 2023** certificate has not been deployed to the Secure Boot DB yet.

The original Windows UEFI CA 2011 certificates expire on **June 27, 2026**. After expiration, devices without the 2023 certificate will no longer receive Secure Boot security updates and may fail to boot with updated boot managers.

Current UEFI CA 2023 status: `{{uefiCA2023Status}}`

remediation.title: Deploy UEFI CA 2023 certificate
steps: Deploy KB5036210 via Windows Update or WSUS
Use Intune policy to enable Secure Boot DB update (Windows UEFI CA 2023)
Alternatively, use the WinCS CLI tool from Microsoft for manual deployment
Verify deployment: check registry HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\SecureBoot\Servicing\UEFICA2023Status = 'Updated'
After DB update: ensure the boot manager is updated to a 2023-signed version via monthly cumulative update

relatedDocs.title: Microsoft: Secure Boot certificates expire June 2026

rules.title: AutoLogon enabled on device
rules.description: Fires when the AutoLogon analyzer reports an active automatic sign-in (AutoAdminLogon or ForceAutoLogon set in Winlogon). AutoLogon can be a legitimate kiosk or shared-device configuration, but it is also a classic way to automate enrollment or OOBE, so an active AutoLogon during Autopilot is worth surfacing.
rules.explanation:
This device has **automatic sign-in (AutoLogon) enabled** in the registry (`HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\Winlogon`). The `AutoAdminLogon` and/or `ForceAutoLogon` value is set, so Windows will log a user in without prompting for credentials.

This is legitimate for kiosk and shared-device scenarios, but it is also a common technique to automate enrollment or OOBE. If AutoLogon was not part of your intended deployment, treat it as a possible manipulation: review the configured default user, the source that wrote the keys (provisioning script, Win32 app, or the Sysinternals Autologon tool), and whether the account is more privileged than the kiosk use case requires.

**Sysinternals Autologon:** when the evidence includes `sysinternals_suspected`, the AutoLogon password is stored as an encrypted **LSA secret** instead of a plaintext `DefaultPassword` registry value — the signature of the Sysinternals Autologon tool (AutoLogon enabled, a default user set, and no registry password). That is a deliberate, tool-assisted setup: still legitimate for an approved kiosk, but a stronger indicator of intent than a flag left behind by accident (the rule raises its confidence accordingly).

Review the `autologon_analysis` event in the session timeline for the exact Winlogon values (default user / domain, AutoLogon count, whether a Sysinternals LSA-secret password is suspected).

remediation.title: Confirm AutoLogon is intended
steps: Open the autologon_analysis event in the session timeline and review the default user/domain and AutoLogon flags
Confirm whether AutoLogon is part of an approved kiosk / shared-device deployment
If unexpected, identify what wrote the Winlogon keys (provisioning script, Win32 app, or Sysinternals Autologon)

remediation.title: Disable AutoLogon if not required
steps: Set HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\Winlogon\AutoAdminLogon to 0
Remove DefaultPassword (and any Sysinternals LSA secret) if present
For kiosk scenarios, prefer Assigned Access / kiosk profiles over raw AutoLogon where possible

remediation.title: Run deliberate kiosks? Use the allow-list instead
steps: If AutoLogon is an approved kiosk configuration, configure the template rule ANALYZE-SEC-004 with your expected kiosk account name(s)
Then disable this rule (ANALYZE-SEC-002) so approved kiosk AutoLogon stays quiet while AutoLogon for unexpected users still warns

relatedDocs.title: Microsoft: Configure Windows to automatically sign in (AutoLogon)

rules.title: AutoLogon plaintext password stored in registry
rules.description: Fires when the AutoLogon analyzer reports a DefaultPassword value present in Winlogon. A plaintext AutoLogon password stored in the registry is readable by any local administrator and is a credential exposure — more severe than AutoLogon alone (the Sysinternals Autologon tool deliberately avoids this by storing the secret in the LSA instead).
rules.explanation:
This device has a **plaintext AutoLogon password stored in the registry** (`DefaultPassword` under `HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\Winlogon`). This value is readable by any local administrator and exposes the AutoLogon account's credentials in clear text.

Even when AutoLogon itself is intended (e.g. a kiosk), the password should never be left as a registry value. The Sysinternals Autologon tool stores the secret as an encrypted LSA secret precisely to avoid this; a plaintext `DefaultPassword` usually means a script or manual edit wrote the credentials directly.

Note: the agent only reports the *presence* of this value — it never reads or transmits the password itself.

remediation.title: Remove the plaintext password
steps: Delete the DefaultPassword value under HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\Winlogon
If AutoLogon is still required, reconfigure it with the Sysinternals Autologon tool so the secret is stored as an encrypted LSA secret rather than a registry value
Rotate the exposed account's password, since it may have been readable on disk

remediation.title: Reduce blast radius
steps: Verify the AutoLogon account is the least-privileged account that satisfies the use case (not a domain or local admin where avoidable)
For kiosk scenarios, prefer Assigned Access / kiosk profiles over a raw AutoLogon credential

rules.title: AutoLogon enabled for an unexpected user
rules.description: Template variant of the AutoLogon warning with a kiosk allow-list. Fires when AutoLogon is enabled for a default user that is NOT on your configured list of approved kiosk / shared-device accounts. Disabled until configured: set your allowed account name(s), then disable the broad ANALYZE-SEC-002 so deliberate kiosk AutoLogon stops raising a warning while unexpected AutoLogon still does.
rules.explanation:
AutoLogon is enabled on this device for a user that is **not on your approved kiosk allow-list**. AutoLogon is legitimate for kiosk and shared-device scenarios, so this rule only warns when the configured default user differs from the account(s) you expect — letting you keep deliberate kiosk AutoLogon quiet while still surfacing unexpected automatic sign-in.

To use this rule: configure the allow-list with your approved kiosk account name(s) (comma-separated, case-insensitive exact match against the Winlogon `DefaultUserName`), then disable the broad **ANALYZE-SEC-002 — AutoLogon enabled on device** rule so it does not also fire on your approved kiosk devices.

Note: an AutoLogon with no explicit default user (e.g. ForceAutoLogon only) is treated as unexpected and will warn.

remediation.title: Confirm the AutoLogon user
steps: Open the autologon_analysis event in the session timeline and check the default user/domain
If the user is an approved kiosk account, add it to this rule's allow-list
If the user is unexpected, treat it as a possible enrollment/OOBE manipulation and investigate what wrote the Winlogon keys

remediation.title: Disable AutoLogon if not required
steps: Set HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\Winlogon\AutoAdminLogon to 0
Remove DefaultPassword (and any Sysinternals LSA secret) if present
For kiosk scenarios, prefer Assigned Access / kiosk profiles over raw AutoLogon where possible

relatedDocs.title: Microsoft: Configure Windows to automatically sign in (AutoLogon)

templateVariables.name: allowed_autologon_users
templateVariables.label: Allowed AutoLogon users (kiosk accounts)
templateVariables.description: Comma-separated list of approved kiosk / shared-device account names that are expected to use AutoLogon (case-insensitive exact match against the Winlogon DefaultUserName, e.g. 'KioskUser' or 'Reception,LobbyPC'). AutoLogon for any other user raises a warning.

rules.title: Unexpected Provisioning Package
rules.description: Detects provisioning packages (PPKG) that are not on the built-in allow-list. The agent emits one or more provisioning_package_scan events whose artifacts[] arrays list every PPKG (registry package, .ppkg file, or Recovery\Customizations residue; a large set is split across several events). This rule iterates that array across all of them and fires for any artifact whose identity does not match the allow-list regex. The default allow-list covers the Windows OS-inbox packages that ship on every device (Cosa, Power.EnergyEstimationEngine, Power.Settings, SecureStart) so a clean device stays quiet. To allow your own vendor/recovery packages, enable the template rule ANALYZE-SEC-006, extend its allow-list, then disable this rule.
rules.explanation:
A provisioning package (PPKG) was detected that is NOT on the allow-list.

- **Unexpected package:** `{{identity}}`

The agent reports every PPKG it finds in `provisioning_package_scan` event(s) (see their `artifacts` array(s) for the full list, plus file metadata and best-effort content indicators; large sets span several events). This rule ignores the Windows OS-inbox packages that ship on every device (Cosa, Power.EnergyEstimationEngine, Power.Settings, SecureStart) and only surfaces the rest.

Provisioning packages can apply local accounts, certificates, Wi-Fi/VPN profiles, applications (MSI/EXE) and scripts. They are legitimate in bulk-enrollment and OEM-recovery scenarios, but a PPKG appearing on a device that is expected to be "pure Autopilot" can indicate tampering.

**To stop alerting on a known-good package:** enable the template rule **ANALYZE-SEC-006**, add the package to its allow-list regex, then disable this rule. ANALYZE-SEC-006 then only fires for packages outside your extended allow-list.

remediation.title: Verify the provisioning package is expected
steps: Open the provisioning_package_scan event and review the artifacts array (identity, source, file path, ownerType/installTime for registry packages)
Confirm whether the package matches a known source: bulk-enrollment PPKG, OEM recovery package, or an internal provisioning workflow
Cross-check the device model/vendor — OEM recovery PPKGs are common and usually benign
If the package is unexpected for a pure-Autopilot device, treat it as a potential manipulation and investigate how it was applied (USB, Recovery\Customizations, image)
To allow known-good packages going forward, enable ANALYZE-SEC-006, extend the allow-list, then disable this rule

rules.title: Unexpected Provisioning Package (Custom Allow-List)
rules.description: Template version of ANALYZE-SEC-005 with a customizable allow-list. Iterates the provisioning_package_scan artifacts[] array and fires for any PPKG whose identity is not on YOUR allow-list regex. The placeholder ships with the same OS-inbox defaults as ANALYZE-SEC-005 — extend it with your own vendor/recovery packages. This rule is OFF by default: enable it, set your allow-list, then disable ANALYZE-SEC-005 so only packages outside your list alert.
rules.explanation:
A provisioning package (PPKG) was detected whose identity is NOT on your configured allow-list.

- **Unexpected package:** `{{identity}}`

The `identity` value combines a package's name, .ppkg file name, ownerType and packageId, so your allow-list regex can match an approved package by any of these. The default allow-list already covers the Windows OS-inbox packages (Cosa, Power.EnergyEstimationEngine, Power.Settings, SecureStart) — extend it with your own vendor/recovery packages.

If this package should be allowed, add it to the allow-list regex (the 'value' of the 'ppkg_not_allowlisted' condition). If it is genuinely unexpected on a pure-Autopilot device, treat it as a potential manipulation and investigate how it was applied.

remediation.title: Triage the unexpected package
steps: Open the provisioning_package_scan event and review the artifacts array for this package's identity and source
Decide: is this a known-good package that should be allow-listed, or genuinely unexpected?

If known-good: the allow-list is anchored (^(?:...)\b), so insert a new alternative INSIDE the group, before the closing ')' — e.g. ...|SecureStart\.Settings|Acme\.Recovery)\b. Appending after the ) or \b will not match.
If unexpected: investigate the application path (USB media, C:\Recovery\Customizations residue, custom image) and the device's chain of custody

remediation.title: Activate the custom allow-list workflow (2-step)
steps: Enable this rule and extend the allow-list regex by inserting your approved packages as alternatives inside the anchored group (before the closing ')'), case-insensitive
Verify it behaves as expected on a known device
Disable the built-in rule ANALYZE-SEC-005 so only packages outside your allow-list alert going forward

templateVariables.name: allowed_packages
templateVariables.label: Allowed provisioning packages (regex)
templateVariables.description: Case-insensitive regex matched against each package's identity (name | fileName | ownerType | packageId). Packages matching this pattern are treated as approved and do NOT alert. The default is anchored (^...) so it matches only at the START of the identity — extend it by adding an alternative inside the group, e.g. add '|Acme\.Recovery' before the closing ')'.

rules.title: Provisioning Package Scan Incomplete
rules.description: Fires when the provisioning-package scan hit a per-source collection cap (too many .ppkg files, registry packages, or Recovery\Customizations residue files to capture them all). When that happens the allow-list rules (ANALYZE-SEC-005/006) could not evaluate every package, so a non-allow-listed package may have gone unseen. A device with that many provisioning packages is itself unusual and worth a manual look.
rules.explanation:
The provisioning-package scan was incomplete: the device had more provisioning packages than the agent captures per source, so a per-source cap was hit (see `ppkgFilesTruncated` / `packagesTruncated` / `recoveryFilesTruncated` on the `provisioning_package_scan` event).

Because not every package was captured, the allow-list rules (ANALYZE-SEC-005/006) may NOT have evaluated all of them — a non-allow-listed package could be present but unseen. A device carrying this many provisioning packages is also unusual on its own and warrants a manual review.

remediation.title: Review the device manually
steps: Open the provisioning_package_scan event and check which counts exceeded the cap (ppkgFileCount / packageCount vs the *Truncated flags)
Inspect the device directly: C:\ProgramData\Microsoft\Provisioning, C:\Windows\Provisioning, C:\Recovery\Customizations, and HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Provisioning\Packages
Confirm whether the large number of provisioning packages is expected for this device/model (e.g. an OEM image) or anomalous
If anomalous, treat it as a potential manipulation and investigate the device's chain of custody
